coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer nanospheres: An effective fluorescent sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of chromium(VI) ion
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Xun
Sun, Huaiyu
Yang, Siwei
Zhao, Shizhen
Liao, Fang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we demonstrated a fluorescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer sensing system, which was synthesized by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method. The copolymer was first used as fluorescent probe for the detection of Chromium(VI) ion (Cr6+) and showed high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection limit was 1×10−11 M. It showed excellent linear relationships in wide range of 7×10−11–6×10−10 M. Moreover, the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) to the detection system could successfully combine with Cr6+ to form metal chelates, making the fluorescence recovery of o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer. What is important, the prepared process had no addition of initiating agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: o-Phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer
Fluorescent sensoring
Chromium(VI) ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(A) Low and (B) high magnification SEM images of OMCP. (C) Particle size distribution of OMCP. (D) UV–vis absorption (blue line), PLE (black line λ
ex =356nm) and PL (black line λ
em =460nm) spectra of OMCP aqueous solution. (E) The FTIR spectrum of OMCP, oPD and mPD. (F) EDS spectrum of OMCP. Inset: element concentrations of C, N and O of OMCP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL emission spectrum of OMCP (black line), in the presence of Cr6+ (red line), both Cr6+ and EDTA (blue line). Inset: Photographs of above three solution under visible light. The final concentrations of Cr6+, EDTA, are 5×10−5 M, 6×10−5 M, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) PL emission spectrum of OMCP in the presence of different concentrations of Cr6+ (0, 5, 7, 10, 12, 14, 16,18, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 65, 70 (×10−11 M), 25°C). (B) Linear fitting of The Stern–Volmer plot of the PL peak versus different Cr6+.

(A) Stability of the OMCP in the different time under visible light. F 0 and F are PL intensities in 0h and specify time, respectively. (B) Stability of the OMCP in the different PH at 25°C. F 0 and F are PL intensities of OMCP solution and specify PH, respectively. (C) Stability of the OMCP in the different concentrations of NaCl (1, 10−1, 10−2, 10−3, 10−4, 10−5 M).

(A) PL intensity of OMCP (25°C) with the absence and presence of Cr6+ and different metal ions. F 0 and F are OMCP fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The concentrations of all metal ions is 5×10−5 M.
A schematic diagram of the Mechanism of the Detection of Cr6+ by the Fluorescent OMCP.
Analytical performance comparison table about the detection for Cr6+.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer nanospheres: An effective fluorescent sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of chromium(VI) ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control, Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Industry, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control, Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Industry, China West Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Technique College of Southwest Peteoleum University, Nanchong 637002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Applied Technique College of Southwest Peteoleum University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we demonstrated a fluorescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer sensing system, which was synthesized by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method. The copolymer was first used as fluorescent probe for the detection of Chromium(VI) ion (Cr6+) and showed high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection limit was 1×10−11 M. It showed excellent linear relationships in wide range of 7×10−11–6×10−10 M. Moreover, the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) to the detection system could successfully combine with Cr6+ to form metal chelates, making the fluorescence recovery of o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer. What is important, the prepared process had no addition of initiating agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted a great deal of attention due to their high emission rates, extraordinary fluorescence brightness, excellent photostability, high quantum yields and large absorption cross-sections as fluorescent probes [1–8]. Compared with other polymer dots (Pdots) which primarily consist of π-conjugated polymers, fluorescent copolymer dots could be prepared easily, low-cost and low cytotoxicity, so it obtain considerable research in various fields [9–12]. Wu et al. reported highly fluorescent semiconducting polymer dots and applied them to biology and medicine [13]. Chiu et al. reported stable functionalization of small semiconducting polymer dots via covalent cross-linking and their application for specific cellular imaging [14]. Although Pdots had been widely studied, it was still a challenge to develop new Pdots with various properties which could be applied in detection of heavy metal ions.

With the development of economies in recent years, contamination of the environment has been an important concern throughout the world, especially for heavy metal ions such as Chromium(VI) ion (Cr6+), Silver ion (Ag+), Cadmium ion (Cd2+), Mercury ion (Hg2+) and Lead ion (Pb2+) [15–22]. Heavy metal ions in drinking water have become a most serious and urgent global concern due to their high toxicity, and they could also cause severe environmental and human health issues [23,24]. Among these metal ions, Cr is a transition metal most commonly found in the environment and which has various oxidation stages ranging from 0 to VI. Only two of them, chromium(III) and chromium(VI), are, however, stable enough to occur in the environment. Chromium(VI) is 100–1000 times more toxic than chromium(III) and seldom found in potable water [20,25–27]. Chromium(VI) has been classified as a human carcinogen by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the total chromium content in drinking water should not exceed a maximum limit of 0.1ppm [17,22]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set the safety limit of Cr6+ in drinking water is 0.05ppm [27].

By now, various methods for removing Cr6+ from nature water and industrial waste have been developed, including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), photoelectrochemical methods, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), fluorescence spectrophotometer and electro deposition [28–30]. Fluorescence spectrophotometer is one of the most attractive methods for its simplicity, low-cost, high sensitivity and effectiveness. Various fluorescence sensors have also been designed for Cr6+ detection, such as organic fluorophores [31], quantum dots [32] and metal nanoparticles [33]. However, using conjugated polymers as chemical sensors for detecting heavy-metal ions in aqueous media has rarely been reported. Accessible fluorescent conjugated polymer materials for sensitive and selective Cr6+ detection need to be developed.

In this paper, we demonstrated a novel fluorescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer (OMCP) nanosphere sensing system based on the fluorescence quenching. Because o-phenylenediamine and m-phenylenediamine can contain active –NH2 group, we synthetized the OMCP containing rich –NH2 group, and it could strongly interact with metal ions to form the stable metal complexes. It was first used as an ultrasensitive fluorescence probe for Cr6+ with wide detection range. The detection limit was 1×10−11 M at room temperature. With the increase of concentrations of Cr6+ in the range of 5×10−11–7×10−10 M, the PL intensity of the OMCP was proportionately decreased. Notably, the addition of chelating agent (such as EDTA) into the sensing system could successfully combine with Cr6+ to form metal chelates, which made the OMCP out of sensing system. That is, fluorescence of OMCP can be recovered. To our best knowledge, this fluorescent OMCP was first synthesized. The Scheme 1 showed a schematic diagram how to detect Cr6+ by the fluorescent OMCP.

o-phenylenediamine (oPD, 99%), m-phenylenediamine (mPD, 99.5%), KCl (99.5%), NaCl (99.5%), Co(NO3)2 (99%), ZnSO4 (99%), AgNO3 (99%), Pb(NO3)2 (99%), NiSO4 (99%), HgCl2 (99%), FeCl3 (99%), FeCl2 (99%), CaCl2 (99%) and CrO3 (99%) were purchased from Kelong (Chengdu, China), and used without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system.

Typically, 0.05M oPD and 0.05M mPD were mixed as volume ratio is 1:1, then the mixture was transferred into a 20mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 15h, and then cooling to room temperature naturally. A yellow transparent solution was contained by removing the large dots through centrifugation. The black precipitates were wasted with water by centrifugation twice, and the resulting precipitates were dried in vacuum for further use.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were made on a JSM-6510LV (Rigaku, Japan) scanning electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 20kV. UV–vis spectra were obtained on a UV2550 Spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was performed on a Nicolet 6700 (resolution 0.4cm−1). Fluorescent emission and excitation spectra were recorded on a FL0912μ000 cary Eclipse at room temperature (25°C).

Quantum yield (φ) was calculated by the following equation:
φ =
φ
R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
2
η R 2
where I represented the measured integrated emission intensity (exission wavelength of 356nm), η was the refractive index of the solvent, A was the optical density measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer, which was limited to less than 0.1. The subscript R referred to the reference standard with a known φ [34]. Absolute values were calculated using the standard reference sample that had a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value. Here, we chose RhB as a standard (RhB in ethanol solution, φR =0.68). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. The yield was about 30%.

We explored the feasibility of product for detection of Cr6+. It was found that the OMCP showed a strong photoluminescence (PL) peak in the absence of Cr6+ ( Fig. 2, black line). However, the PL intensity of OMCP was obviously decreased when adding Cr6+ (Fig. 2, red line), indicating OMCP could strongly interact with Cr6+ and result in the fluorescence quenching of OMCP. It could be clearly found that the recovery of PL intensity of the OMCP in the presence of Cr6+ when the metal ion chelator EDTA was added (Fig. 2, blue line). The recovery of PL intensity of the OMCP might be due to forming of metal chelates between Cr6+ and EDTA. However, Cr6+ could not completely quench the PL of OMCP and the PL of OMCP could not fully recover, the potential reason was that Cr6+ concentration was too low.

To further reveal the feasibility of OMCP for detection of Cr6+, we observed the change of PL peak of OMCP after adding different concentrations of Cr6+. The PL peaks were measured after adding different concentrations of Cr6+ 30min late. With the increase of concentrations of Cr6+ in the range of 5×10−11–7×10−10 M, the PL intensity of the OMCP was proportionately decreased, as shown in Fig. 3A. The reason of quenching progress was presumably via electron or energy transfer. Strong coordination interactions between Cr6+ and the surface –NH2 groups of OMCP result in the fluorescence quenching of OMCP, suggesting Cr6+ combine with OMCP form the stable metal complexes. Fig. 3B showed a linear Stern–Volmer equation in the different concentrations of Cr6+ in the range of 7×10−11–6×10−10 M. The fluorescence quenching data follows the Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
/ F − 1 =
K
S V
C
where Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, c is the analyte (Cr6+) concentration and F 0, F are PL intensities of OMCP in the absence and presence of Cr6+, respectively. Fig. 3B showed excellent linear relationships in wide range of 7×10−11–6×10−10 M, and the correlation coefficients (r 2) and Ksv are 0.99774 and 0.0028, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr6+, at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, was estimated to be 1×10−11 M, much lower than limit of Cr6+ in drinking water set by the WHO (0.05ppm). Compared with previous reports, as-prepared OMCP have higher sensitivity and lower LOD for detection of Cr6+. The materials for detection of Cr6+ were shown in Table 1 [39–44]. Above results suggested that the OMCP has potential applications for recognition of Cr6+ in detection of heavy metal ions in water.

The feasibility of as-prepared products was very important in practical applications. Therefore, before using OMCP as a fluorescent probe for detection of Cr6+, we took three different influencing factors into consideration to investigate the stability of OMCP, namely visible light irradiate, different PH values and concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 4A, we could see PL intensities of OMCP did not obviously change under visible light for more than a week, indicating high stability of the OMCP after continuous light irradiation. In Fig. 4B, we could see the PL intensities of OMCP had only a slight change in a wide range of pH values (pH=1–11). Moreover, we could find PL intensity of OMCP essentially keep unchanged in Fig. 4C with the increase of concentration of NaCl in range of 10−5–1M, indicating the OMCP had better stability under a high ionic strength environment. As such, OMCP had a good stability under various extreme conditions.

To uncover whether OMCP had higher selectivity and sensitivity for Cr6+, we chose various metal ions as contrast ions, such as Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Ni2+. The concentration of each metal ion kept the same, and all metal ions were tested under the same environment conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, it was clearly seen that the PL intensity of OMCP have change when these metal ions was added into OMCP solution. The OMCP containing rich –NH2 group, could strongly interact with metal ions to form stable metal complexes. When Cr6+ was added into OMCP solution, the PL peak of OMCP was obviously decreased compared with addition of the other metal ions. This phenomenon might be due to –NH2 group with stronger reducibility, resulting in higher state of metal reduction for lower state of metal. Cr6+ was much easier to get the electronic, and thus OMCP had stronger combination with Cr6+ than the other metal ions.

In summary, we demonstrated a novel fluorescent o-phenylenediamine–m-phenylenediamine copolymer (OMCP) nanosphere sensing system based on the fluorescence quenching. The copolymer was synthesized by a facile and one-step hydrothermal method. It was first used as an ultrasensitive fluorescence probe for Cr6+ with wide detection range. This fluorescent copolymer showed high sensitivity and selectivity for Cr6+. The detection limit was 1×10−11 M. Furthermore, the addition of EDTA into the detection system could successfully mask Cr6+, resulting in the recovery of PL intensity of OMCP. The process to prepare OMCP was easy, low-cost and low cytotoxicity. More importantly, polymerization process was no addition of initiating agent. Given above advantages, we believe that the OMCP would have many potential applications in many fields.
